name: Mantle of Invisibility
description: The Mantle of Invisibility, a powerful artifact deeply embedded in Welsh folklore, is a magical cloak capable of rendering its wearer unseen. Its origins are often shrouded in mystery, with some tales attributing its creation to powerful sorcerers or ancient deities. The mantle's power extends beyond mere concealment; it can also provide protection from harm and enhance the wearer's stealth. It is a symbol of cunning, strategy, and the ability to operate unseen, making it a coveted item among heroes and villains alike. The mantle is not merely a tool for deception, but a symbol of power, allowing its user to influence events from the shadows. Legends speak of its use in battles, political intrigues, and quests for hidden knowledge. The Mantle of Invisibility is more than just a cloak; it's a key to unlocking hidden potential and manipulating the world around you, unseen and unheard. The effectiveness of the Mantle is sometimes tied to the wearer's intent, amplifying good or evil deeds. Its magic is subtle and pervasive, influencing not only visibility but also perception. The mantle's presence in Welsh narratives underscores the importance of seeing beyond the surface and recognizing the hidden forces that shape reality. It embodies the concept of the unseen world influencing the seen, a core theme in Welsh mythology.
subtitle: Cloak of unseen power and influence
origin: Created by a powerful sorcerer or gifted by a deity.
currentLocation: Unknown, rumored to be hidden in Snowdonia.

keyMyths.title: Pryderi's Disappearance
keyMyths.description: Pryderi, using a similar magical concealment, was tricked and disappeared. This highlights the dangers of relying solely on invisibility.

keyMyths.title: The Hunt for the Twrch Trwyth
keyMyths.description: While not explicitly the Mantle, similar concepts of magical concealment were used during the legendary hunt, aiding in stealth and surprise.

sources.author: Lady Charlotte Guest
sources.description: A collection of early Welsh tales, providing context for magical items.
sources.text: A collection of early Welsh tales, providing context for magical items.

sources.author: John Rhys
sources.description: Explores various aspects of Welsh folklore, including magical artifacts.
